content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:94f70d80

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable shopping context and preferences plus reusable decision guidance/rationale from a forced past-chat ingestion.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable USER entity; no existing candidates are available.
- mention=West Elm; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=West Elm; reason=Concrete brand/store the user plans to visit and compare; no existing candidates.
- mention=Crate & Barrel; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Crate & Barrel; reason=Concrete brand/store the user plans to visit and compare; no existing candidates.
- mention=Crate and Barrel; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Crate & Barrel; reason=Surface form clearly refers to Crate & Barrel, but no existing entity id is available, so create the canonical entity rather than add an alias.
- mention=IKEA; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=IKEA; reason=Concrete retailer/brand discussed as a shopping destination and product source; no existing candidates.
- mention=coffee tables with storage; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=coffee tables with storage; reason=Reusable product category central to later preference and goal claims.
- mention=IKEA bookshelf; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=IKEA bookshelf; reason=Concrete product category/object the user assembled; useful as object of an experience claim.
- mention=online reviews and ratings; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=online reviews and ratings; reason=Reusable research source/concept for later claims about the user's shopping plan.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|West Elm|Crate & Barrel|IKEA|coffee tables with storage|IKEA bookshelf
        text_queries=coffee table with storage shopping preferences wooden top glass top West Elm Crate Barrel IKEA online reviews|IKEA bookshelf assembled 4 hours
        predicate_hints=prefers|has_experience_with|metric_observation|has_goal

Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 19:23
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on coffee tables with storage space. Do you have any suggestions or should I check out specific brands?
assistant: A coffee table with storage space is a fantastic investment for any living room! It's a great way to keep clutter at bay while still having a stylish and functional piece of furniture. I'd be happy to help you with some recommendations and suggestions.

**Types of Coffee Tables with Storage:**

1. **Lift-Top Coffee Tables**: These have a hinged top that lifts up to reveal a storage compartment underneath.
2. **Drawer Coffee Tables**: These have drawers or shelves that slide out to store items.
3. **Ottoman Coffee Tables**: These have a storage ottoman built into the base of the table.
4. **Shelf Coffee Tables**: These have open shelves or cubbies for storing books, decorations, or other items.

**Popular Brands:**

1. **West Elm**: Known for their modern and stylish designs, West Elm offers a range of coffee tables with storage.
2. ** Crate & Barrel**: This brand offers a variety of coffee tables with storage, including lift-top and drawer styles.
3. **IKEA**: IKEA is a great option for affordable and functional coffee tables with storage, like the popular LACK series.
4. **Pottery Barn**: Pottery Barn offers a range of coffee tables with storage, including rustic and traditional styles.
5. **Wayfair**: Wayfair is an online retailer that carries a wide selection of coffee tables with storage from various brands.

**Things to Consider:**

1. **Measure Your Space**: Make sure to measure your living room and the space where the coffee table will go to ensure the one you choose fits comfortably.
2. **Storage Needs**: Think about what you want to store in your coffee table. Do you need space for books, games, or decorative items?
3. **Style and Material**: Consider the style and material of your living room furniture and choose a coffee table that fits in with the aesthetic.
4. **Budget**: Set a budget and stick to it. Coffee tables with storage can range from affordable to very expensive.

**Some Specific Recommendations:**

1. **West Elm's Storage Coffee Table**: A modern, lift-top coffee table with a sleek design.
2. **Crate & Barrel's Dawson Coffee Table**: A stylish, drawer-style coffee table with a rustic wood finish.
3. **IKEA's LACK Coffee Table**: An affordable, compact coffee table with storage and a modern design.
4. **Pottery Barn's Benchwright Coffee Table**: A rustic, storage coffee table with a bench-style design.

I hope these recommendations and suggestions help you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!
user: I've been leaning towards a wooden top, but now I'm considering the glass top option too. Do you think a glass top would be more prone to scratches or fingerprints compared to wood? Also, by the way, I just assembled an IKEA bookshelf recently and it took me 4 hours, which wasn't too bad.
assistant: Great choice on considering both wood and glass top options!

**Glass Top vs. Wood Top: Scratch Resistance and Fingerprints**

Glass tops are generally more resistant to scratches than wood tops, especially if you opt for a tempered glass or low-iron glass. These types of glass are more durable and less prone to scratches or breakage. However, glass tops can still scratch or crack if subjected to heavy impact or sharp objects.

On the other hand, wood tops can scratch or dent more easily, especially if you have a softwood or a wood finish that's not very durable. However, wood tops can be refinished or repaired if scratched, which is not always possible with glass tops.

Regarding fingerprints, glass tops tend to show fingerprints more easily than wood tops. This is because glass is a smooth, non-porous surface that can attract fingerprints and smudges. Wood tops, on the other hand, have a natural texture that can help hide fingerprints.

**Maintenance and Cleaning**

Both glass and wood tops require regular cleaning to maintain their appearance. Glass tops are generally easier to clean, as they can be wiped down with a damp cloth and a mild cleaning solution. Wood tops may require more care, as they can be damaged by harsh chemicals or excessive moisture. You may need to use a wood-specific cleaner and polish to maintain the finish.

**Your IKEA Assembly Experience**

 Congrats on assembling your IKEA bookshelf in 4 hours! That's a great accomplishment! IKEA furniture can be challenging to assemble, but the sense of accomplishment when you're done is rewarding. It's also a great way to save money and develop your DIY skills.

If you're considering an IKEA coffee table with storage, you might want to look into their MALM or LACK series, which are popular options with storage compartments.

**Additional Tips**

When choosing between a glass top and a wood top, consider the following:

* Lifestyle: If you have young children or pets, a glass top might be a better option, as it's easier to clean and less prone to damage. If you're looking for a more rustic or traditional look, wood might be the way to go.
* Decor style: Glass tops work well with modern, minimalist, or industrial decor styles, while wood tops fit well with traditional, rustic, or coastal styles.
* Budget: Glass tops can be more expensive than wood tops, especially if you opt for high-quality, tempered glass.

I hope this helps you make a more informed decision!
user: I was thinking of heading to West Elm this weekend to check out their selection in person. Do you think I should also stop by Crate & Barrel to compare their coffee tables with storage? And how do you think I should prioritize the factors I'm considering, such as style, material, budget, and storage needs?
assistant: **Visiting West Elm and Crate & Barrel**

Absolutely! Visiting both West Elm and Crate & Barrel is a great idea. Seeing the coffee tables with storage in person will give you a better sense of the quality, design, and functionality. You can also get a feel for the materials, finishes, and overall aesthetic of each piece. Comparing the two stores' offerings will help you make a more informed decision.

**Prioritizing Factors**

When considering the factors, prioritize them based on your needs and preferences. Here's a suggested order:

1. **Storage needs**: Think about what you need to store and how much space you require. If you have specific items in mind, such as large books or decorative items, make sure the coffee table can accommodate them.
2. **Budget**: Set a realistic budget and stick to it. This will help you narrow down your options and avoid overspending.
3. **Style**: Consider the overall aesthetic you want to achieve in your living room. Do you prefer modern, traditional, rustic, or a mix? Ensure the coffee table fits with your desired style.
4. **Material**: Think about the material's durability, maintenance, and overall look. Wood, glass, and metal each have their pros and cons, so weigh these factors carefully.

By prioritizing your needs and preferences, you'll be able to make a more informed decision and find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.

**Additional Tips for Your Store Visits**

* Take measurements of your living room and the space where the coffee table will go. This will help you visualize the piece in your space.
* Bring a list of your priorities and questions to ask the sales associates.
* Don't be afraid to ask about the materials, construction, and durability of the coffee tables.
* Take photos of the coffee tables that catch your eye, and consider taking a photo of the price tag and product information to reference later.
* If possible, try to visit both stores on the same day to compare the options more easily.

By following these tips, you'll be well-prepared to find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room. Happy shopping!
user: I think I'll also stop by IKEA while I'm out to check out their coffee tables with storage, since I had a decent experience assembling that bookshelf. Do you think their coffee tables would be similarly easy to assemble?
assistant: **IKEA Coffee Tables with Storage**

IKEA is a great option to consider for coffee tables with storage, especially since you've had a positive experience with their bookshelf. Their coffee tables are often affordable, functional, and stylish.

**Assembly Experience**

IKEA's coffee tables with storage are likely to have a similar assembly experience to their bookshelves. They're designed to be DIY-friendly, with clear instructions and minimal tools required. However, the complexity of the assembly process may vary depending on the specific model you choose.

Some IKEA coffee tables with storage, like the LACK series, are relatively simple to assemble and might take around 1-2 hours to put together. Others, like the MALM series, might be more complex and require 2-4 hours of assembly time.

**Tips for Assembling IKEA Coffee Tables**

To ensure a smooth assembly experience, follow these tips:

1. **Read the instructions carefully**: IKEA's instructions are generally clear, but it's essential to read them thoroughly to avoid mistakes.
2. **Prepare your workspace**: Clear a flat surface, gather all the necessary tools, and have a cup of coffee (or two) ready to help you focus.
3. **Sort the parts**: Organize the components and hardware before starting the assembly process to avoid confusion.
4. **Take your time**: Don't rush the assembly process. Take breaks if needed, and double-check your work as you go.
5. **Use online resources**: If you're stuck, search for online tutorials or YouTube videos specific to your coffee table model.

**Comparing IKEA to West Elm and Crate & Barrel**

When comparing IKEA's coffee tables with storage to those from West Elm and Crate & Barrel, consider the following:

* **Price**: IKEA is generally more affordable, but the quality and materials might not be as high-end as the other two brands.
* **Design**: IKEA's designs are often more minimalist and modern, while West Elm and Crate & Barrel offer a wider range of styles and finishes.
* **Quality**: West Elm and Crate & Barrel tend to use higher-quality materials and construction methods, which may result in a more durable product.

By visiting all three stores, you'll get a better sense of each brand's offerings and can make a more informed decision based on your priorities.

Have fun shopping, and I hope you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!
user: I'm thinking of checking out some online reviews and ratings for the coffee tables I'm interested in, to get a better sense of their quality and durability. Do you think that's a good idea, or should I just rely on the information I gather in-store?
assistant: **Online Reviews and Ratings**

Checking online reviews and ratings is an excellent idea! It's a great way to get a more comprehensive understanding of a product's quality, durability, and performance. Online reviews can provide valuable insights from people who have already purchased and used the product, which can help you make a more informed decision.

**Pros of Online Reviews**

1. **Real-world experience**: Online reviews offer real-world experiences from people who have used the product, which can give you a better understanding of its strengths and weaknesses.
2. **Multiple perspectives**: You can read reviews from multiple sources, including different websites, social media, and forums, to get a well-rounded view of the product.
3. **Long-term performance**: Online reviews can provide insights into how a product holds up over time, which is essential for evaluating durability and quality.
4. **Comparison**: You can easily compare reviews of different products to see how they stack up against each other.

**Cons of Online Reviews**

1. **Biased opinions**: Some reviews might be biased or fake, so it's essential to read multiple reviews from various sources to get a balanced view.
2. **Outliers**: Some reviewers might have had an unusual experience, which might not be representative of the product's typical performance.
3. **Older reviews**: Reviews from several years ago might not reflect the current product or manufacturing process.

**In-Store Experience**

While online reviews are valuable, it's still important to visit the stores and see the products in person. Here's why:

1. **Tactile experience**: You can touch, feel, and examine the product up close, which can give you a better sense of its quality and materials.
2. **Sales associate insights**: Sales associates can provide valuable information about the product, answer questions, and offer comparisons to other products.
3. **Return policy**: You can ask about the return policy and warranty in case you're not satisfied with the product.

**Combining Online and In-Store Research**

To get the best of both worlds, combine your online research with your in-store visit. Here's a suggested approach:

1. **Research online**: Read reviews, ratings, and product descriptions to narrow down your options and create a list of potential products.
2. **Visit the store**: Visit the stores, see the products in person, and ask questions to sales associates.
3. **Verify online findings**: Verify the information you found online and ask about any concerns or issues you read about in reviews.
4. **Make an informed decision**: Make your decision based on your online research, in-store experience, and sales associate insights.

By combining online and in-store research, you'll be well-equipped to make an informed decision and find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.
user: I'll head to West Elm, Crate and Barrel, and IKEA this weekend to check out their coffee tables with storage. I'll also read online reviews and ratings to get a better sense of each product's quality and durability. Thanks for the tips!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm thrilled to have been able to help. It sounds like you have a solid plan in place to find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.

Remember to take your time, ask questions, and don't hesitate to reach out if you need any more advice or guidance. And don't forget to measure your space and bring those measurements with you to the stores!

By combining your in-store visits with online research, you'll be able to make an informed decision and find a coffee table that meets your needs, fits your style, and stays within your budget.

Good luck with your shopping adventure, and I hope you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:94f70d80

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable shopping context and preferences plus reusable decision guidance/rationale from a forced past-chat ingestion.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some recommendations on coffee tables with storage space. Do you have any suggestions or should I check out specific brands?
assistant: A coffee table with storage space is a fantastic investment for any living room! It's a great way to keep clutter at bay while still having a stylish and functional piece of furniture. I'd be happy to help you with some recommendations and suggestions.

**Types of Coffee Tables with Storage:**

1. **Lift-Top Coffee Tables**: These have a hinged top that lifts up to reveal a storage compartment underneath.
2. **Drawer Coffee Tables**: These have drawers or shelves that slide out to store items.
3. **Ottoman Coffee Tables**: These have a storage ottoman built into the base of the table.
4. **Shelf Coffee Tables**: These have open shelves or cubbies for storing books, decorations, or other items.

**Popular Brands:**

1. **West Elm**: Known for their modern and stylish designs, West Elm offers a range of coffee tables with storage.
2. ** Crate & Barrel**: This brand offers a variety of coffee tables with storage, including lift-top and drawer styles.
3. **IKEA**: IKEA is a great option for affordable and functional coffee tables with storage, like the popular LACK series.
4. **Pottery Barn**: Pottery Barn offers a range of coffee tables with storage, including rustic and traditional styles.
5. **Wayfair**: Wayfair is an online retailer that carries a wide selection of coffee tables with storage from various brands.

**Things to Consider:**

1. **Measure Your Space**: Make sure to measure your living room and the space where the coffee table will go to ensure the one you choose fits comfortably.
2. **Storage Needs**: Think about what you want to store in your coffee table. Do you need space for books, games, or decorative items?
3. **Style and Material**: Consider the style and material of your living room furniture and choose a coffee table that fits in with the aesthetic.
4. **Budget**: Set a budget and stick to it. Coffee tables with storage can range from affordable to very expensive.

**Some Specific Recommendations:**

1. **West Elm's Storage Coffee Table**: A modern, lift-top coffee table with a sleek design.
2. **Crate & Barrel's Dawson Coffee Table**: A stylish, drawer-style coffee table with a rustic wood finish.
3. **IKEA's LACK Coffee Table**: An affordable, compact coffee table with storage and a modern design.
4. **Pottery Barn's Benchwright Coffee Table**: A rustic, storage coffee table with a bench-style design.

I hope these recommendations and suggestions help you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!
user: I've been leaning towards a wooden top, but now I'm considering the glass top option too. Do you think a glass top would be more prone to scratches or fingerprints compared to wood? Also, by the way, I just assembled an IKEA bookshelf recently and it took me 4 hours, which wasn't too bad.
assistant: Great choice on considering both wood and glass top options!

**Glass Top vs. Wood Top: Scratch Resistance and Fingerprints**

Glass tops are generally more resistant to scratches than wood tops, especially if you opt for a tempered glass or low-iron glass. These types of glass are more durable and less prone to scratches or breakage. However, glass tops can still scratch or crack if subjected to heavy impact or sharp objects.

On the other hand, wood tops can scratch or dent more easily, especially if you have a softwood or a wood finish that's not very durable. However, wood tops can be refinished or repaired if scratched, which is not always possible with glass tops.

Regarding fingerprints, glass tops tend to show fingerprints more easily than wood tops. This is because glass is a smooth, non-porous surface that can attract fingerprints and smudges. Wood tops, on the other hand, have a natural texture that can help hide fingerprints.

**Maintenance and Cleaning**

Both glass and wood tops require regular cleaning to maintain their appearance. Glass tops are generally easier to clean, as they can be wiped down with a damp cloth and a mild cleaning solution. Wood tops may require more care, as they can be damaged by harsh chemicals or excessive moisture. You may need to use a wood-specific cleaner and polish to maintain the finish.

**Your IKEA Assembly Experience**

 Congrats on assembling your IKEA bookshelf in 4 hours! That's a great accomplishment! IKEA furniture can be challenging to assemble, but the sense of accomplishment when you're done is rewarding. It's also a great way to save money and develop your DIY skills.

If you're considering an IKEA coffee table with storage, you might want to look into their MALM or LACK series, which are popular options with storage compartments.

**Additional Tips**

When choosing between a glass top and a wood top, consider the following:

* Lifestyle: If you have young children or pets, a glass top might be a better option, as it's easier to clean and less prone to damage. If you're looking for a more rustic or traditional look, wood might be the way to go.
* Decor style: Glass tops work well with modern, minimalist, or industrial decor styles, while wood tops fit well with traditional, rustic, or coastal styles.
* Budget: Glass tops can be more expensive than wood tops, especially if you opt for high-quality, tempered glass.

I hope this helps you make a more informed decision!
user: I was thinking of heading to West Elm this weekend to check out their selection in person. Do you think I should also stop by Crate & Barrel to compare their coffee tables with storage? And how do you think I should prioritize the factors I'm considering, such as style, material, budget, and storage needs?
assistant: **Visiting West Elm and Crate & Barrel**

Absolutely! Visiting both West Elm and Crate & Barrel is a great idea. Seeing the coffee tables with storage in person will give you a better sense of the quality, design, and functionality. You can also get a feel for the materials, finishes, and overall aesthetic of each piece. Comparing the two stores' offerings will help you make a more informed decision.

**Prioritizing Factors**

When considering the factors, prioritize them based on your needs and preferences. Here's a suggested order:

1. **Storage needs**: Think about what you need to store and how much space you require. If you have specific items in mind, such as large books or decorative items, make sure the coffee table can accommodate them.
2. **Budget**: Set a realistic budget and stick to it. This will help you narrow down your options and avoid overspending.
3. **Style**: Consider the overall aesthetic you want to achieve in your living room. Do you prefer modern, traditional, rustic, or a mix? Ensure the coffee table fits with your desired style.
4. **Material**: Think about the material's durability, maintenance, and overall look. Wood, glass, and metal each have their pros and cons, so weigh these factors carefully.

By prioritizing your needs and preferences, you'll be able to make a more informed decision and find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.

**Additional Tips for Your Store Visits**

* Take measurements of your living room and the space where the coffee table will go. This will help you visualize the piece in your space.
* Bring a list of your priorities and questions to ask the sales associates.
* Don't be afraid to ask about the materials, construction, and durability of the coffee tables.
* Take photos of the coffee tables that catch your eye, and consider taking a photo of the price tag and product information to reference later.
* If possible, try to visit both stores on the same day to compare the options more easily.

By following these tips, you'll be well-prepared to find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room. Happy shopping!
user: I think I'll also stop by IKEA while I'm out to check out their coffee tables with storage, since I had a decent experience assembling that bookshelf. Do you think their coffee tables would be similarly easy to assemble?
assistant: **IKEA Coffee Tables with Storage**

IKEA is a great option to consider for coffee tables with storage, especially since you've had a positive experience with their bookshelf. Their coffee tables are often affordable, functional, and stylish.

**Assembly Experience**

IKEA's coffee tables with storage are likely to have a similar assembly experience to their bookshelves. They're designed to be DIY-friendly, with clear instructions and minimal tools required. However, the complexity of the assembly process may vary depending on the specific model you choose.

Some IKEA coffee tables with storage, like the LACK series, are relatively simple to assemble and might take around 1-2 hours to put together. Others, like the MALM series, might be more complex and require 2-4 hours of assembly time.

**Tips for Assembling IKEA Coffee Tables**

To ensure a smooth assembly experience, follow these tips:

1. **Read the instructions carefully**: IKEA's instructions are generally clear, but it's essential to read them thoroughly to avoid mistakes.
2. **Prepare your workspace**: Clear a flat surface, gather all the necessary tools, and have a cup of coffee (or two) ready to help you focus.
3. **Sort the parts**: Organize the components and hardware before starting the assembly process to avoid confusion.
4. **Take your time**: Don't rush the assembly process. Take breaks if needed, and double-check your work as you go.
5. **Use online resources**: If you're stuck, search for online tutorials or YouTube videos specific to your coffee table model.

**Comparing IKEA to West Elm and Crate & Barrel**

When comparing IKEA's coffee tables with storage to those from West Elm and Crate & Barrel, consider the following:

* **Price**: IKEA is generally more affordable, but the quality and materials might not be as high-end as the other two brands.
* **Design**: IKEA's designs are often more minimalist and modern, while West Elm and Crate & Barrel offer a wider range of styles and finishes.
* **Quality**: West Elm and Crate & Barrel tend to use higher-quality materials and construction methods, which may result in a more durable product.

By visiting all three stores, you'll get a better sense of each brand's offerings and can make a more informed decision based on your priorities.

Have fun shopping, and I hope you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!
user: I'm thinking of checking out some online reviews and ratings for the coffee tables I'm interested in, to get a better sense of their quality and durability. Do you think that's a good idea, or should I just rely on the information I gather in-store?
assistant: **Online Reviews and Ratings**

Checking online reviews and ratings is an excellent idea! It's a great way to get a more comprehensive understanding of a product's quality, durability, and performance. Online reviews can provide valuable insights from people who have already purchased and used the product, which can help you make a more informed decision.

**Pros of Online Reviews**

1. **Real-world experience**: Online reviews offer real-world experiences from people who have used the product, which can give you a better understanding of its strengths and weaknesses.
2. **Multiple perspectives**: You can read reviews from multiple sources, including different websites, social media, and forums, to get a well-rounded view of the product.
3. **Long-term performance**: Online reviews can provide insights into how a product holds up over time, which is essential for evaluating durability and quality.
4. **Comparison**: You can easily compare reviews of different products to see how they stack up against each other.

**Cons of Online Reviews**

1. **Biased opinions**: Some reviews might be biased or fake, so it's essential to read multiple reviews from various sources to get a balanced view.
2. **Outliers**: Some reviewers might have had an unusual experience, which might not be representative of the product's typical performance.
3. **Older reviews**: Reviews from several years ago might not reflect the current product or manufacturing process.

**In-Store Experience**

While online reviews are valuable, it's still important to visit the stores and see the products in person. Here's why:

1. **Tactile experience**: You can touch, feel, and examine the product up close, which can give you a better sense of its quality and materials.
2. **Sales associate insights**: Sales associates can provide valuable information about the product, answer questions, and offer comparisons to other products.
3. **Return policy**: You can ask about the return policy and warranty in case you're not satisfied with the product.

**Combining Online and In-Store Research**

To get the best of both worlds, combine your online research with your in-store visit. Here's a suggested approach:

1. **Research online**: Read reviews, ratings, and product descriptions to narrow down your options and create a list of potential products.
2. **Visit the store**: Visit the stores, see the products in person, and ask questions to sales associates.
3. **Verify online findings**: Verify the information you found online and ask about any concerns or issues you read about in reviews.
4. **Make an informed decision**: Make your decision based on your online research, in-store experience, and sales associate insights.

By combining online and in-store research, you'll be well-equipped to make an informed decision and find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.
user: I'll head to West Elm, Crate and Barrel, and IKEA this weekend to check out their coffee tables with storage. I'll also read online reviews and ratings to get a better sense of each product's quality and durability. Thanks for the tips!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm thrilled to have been able to help. It sounds like you have a solid plan in place to find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room.

Remember to take your time, ask questions, and don't hesitate to reach out if you need any more advice or guidance. And don't forget to measure your space and bring those measurements with you to the stores!

By combining your in-store visits with online research, you'll be able to make an informed decision and find a coffee table that meets your needs, fits your style, and stays within your budget.

Good luck with your shopping adventure, and I hope you find the perfect coffee table with storage for your living room!

Store visits should be supported by room measurements, a priority/question list, inspection of materials and construction, and photos of products plus price tags for later comparison.","tags":["decision_framework","shopping_criteria","furniture"],"title":"Prioritization framework for choosing a coffee table with storage","valid_from":"2023-05-20T19:23:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User had been leaning toward a wooden top but was considering glass.","Glass tops show fingerprints more easily than wood tops.","Wood tops can often be refinished or repaired if scratched."],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"primary"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"I've been leaning towards a wooden top, but now I'm considering the glass top option too.","evidence_reason":"The user explicitly raised the wood-versus-glass decision, and the assistant response supplied reusable comparative trade-offs about scratches, fingerprints, maintenance, and style.","importance":3,"keywords":["wood top","glass top","scratch resistance","fingerprints","maintenance","decor style","budget"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"The note preserves the decision rationale and trade-offs around materials; reducing it to one claim would lose the reusable comparison structure.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"Material trade-off context for the user's coffee table with storage choice"},"summary":"In the coffee table discussion, glass tops were framed as generally more scratch-resistant and easier to clean but more prone to fingerprints/smudges and sometimes more expensive; wood tops may scratch or dent more easily but can hide fingerprints better and may be refinished or repaired. Lifestyle, decor style, maintenance, and budget were the key trade-offs when choosing between them.","tags":["material_tradeoffs","furniture","coffee_table"],"title":"Wood vs glass top trade-offs for the coffee table decision","valid_from":"2023-05-20T19:23:00+03:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User assembled an IKEA bookshelf in 4 hours and considered it not too bad.","User viewed that experience as a reason to consider IKEA coffee tables with storage.","IKEA LACK may take around 1-2 hours to assemble; MALM may take around 2-4 hours."],"confidence":0.9,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"mentioned"}],"evidence_kind":"summarized","evidence_quote":"I had a decent experience assembling that bookshelf.","evidence_reason":"The user connected a prior IKEA bookshelf assembly experience to the decision to include IKEA coffee tables, and the assistant gave model-level assembly expectations and procedure tips.","importance":3,"keywords":["IKEA","bookshelf","4 hours","coffee tables with storage","LACK","MALM","assembly"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This captures reusable context and expectations about IKEA DIY assembly for future furniture choices, not merely the atomic fact that one bookshelf took 4 hours.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"IKEA furniture assembly expectations relevant to the user's furniture-shopping decisions"},"summary":"Because the user assembled an IKEA bookshelf in 4 hours and considered that a decent experience, IKEA coffee tables with storage were treated as plausible DIY-friendly options. The discussed expectation was that simpler IKEA models like LACK might take about 1–2 hours to assemble, while more complex models like MALM might take about 2–4 hours, with assembly eased by reading instructions, sorting parts, preparing workspace, and using model-specific online tutorials if stuck.","tags":["assembly_context","ikea","furniture"],"title":"IKEA coffee table assembly expectations after bookshelf experience","valid_from":"2023-05-20T19:23:00+03:00","valid_to":null}]}
